title: Advanced OOP and SOLID
summary: Design maintainable object-oriented systems using SOLID principles in Python

sections.title: SOLID is a blueprint for maintainable OOP
sections.context: SOLID is an acronym for five design principles that make object-oriented systems easier to maintain, extend, and test. Single Responsibility: a class should have one reason to change — like a chef who only cooks, not also handles accounting. Open/Closed: classes should be open for extension but closed for modification — add features by creating new subclasses, not editing existing ones. Liskov Substitution: subclasses must be substitutable for their parent class without breaking the program. Interface Segregation: many small, specific interfaces are better than one large, general one. Dependency Inversion: depend on abstractions, not concrete implementations. Violating SOLID creates code that's fragile (breaks easily), rigid (hard to change), and immobile (hard to reuse).

sections.title: Violating and fixing the Single Responsibility Principle
sections.context: A Report class that does too much vs properly separated responsibilities

sections.title: Dependency Injection — the key to testable code
sections.context: Dependency Injection (DI) implements the Dependency Inversion principle: instead of a class creating its own dependencies (hard-coded), dependencies are passed in from outside (injected). This decouples the class from specific implementations and makes testing easy — you can inject mock objects. In Python, DI is often done through constructor injection (pass dependencies to __init__). Frameworks like FastAPI use DI extensively. Without DI, testing requires complex setup or monkey-patching. With DI, testing is as simple as passing a mock object. DI doesn't require a framework — manual DI (passing dependencies explicitly) is often clearer and simpler.

sections.title: Liskov Substitution violations are subtle and dangerous
sections.context: A classic violation: a Square class inheriting from Rectangle. Rectangle has set_width(w) and set_height(h). Square overrides these to keep both equal — but now code that works for Rectangle breaks for Square. Wrong: def resize(rect: Rectangle): rect.set_width(5); rect.set_height(10); assert rect.area() == 50 — fails with Square. Fix: don't model Square as subclass of Rectangle. Use a common Shape interface instead. LSP violations often appear when subclasses weaken preconditions or strengthen postconditions of the parent.

sections.title: Your turn
sections.prompt: Redesign a NotificationService class that currently directly creates EmailSender instances inside its methods. Apply Dependency Inversion: create a NotificationSender abstract base class, implement EmailSender and SMSSender as concrete classes, and inject the sender into NotificationService via the constructor. Then write a test that injects a mock sender to verify the service calls send() with the correct message.
hints: What should the __init__ of NotificationService accept to follow Dependency Inversion?
How does injecting dependencies make the class testable without sending real emails?
